item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just out of curiosity, do the 2168-A instances speak Spanish or English?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Elsen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not quite sure who those guys are (it's been a long time since I played it and I never got around to beating it), but now that I think about it, yeah, it does give me kind of an OFF vibe.

Nice article. Not sure I totally get it, but I enjoyed it nonetheless. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Belated answer, sorry!

Peon: mmh. Might be a good idea to explain this in a short footnote?
Not sure about this… A footnote would break the rhythm of the first interview.
OTOH, it is rather important to understand the word, and the meaning is not what I, for one, learned in Spanish class (= peasant, as stated above), so… a bit confusing.
I'll bow to your superior wisdom and let you decide! <grin>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, didn't want to change article until the rating got stable.

So okay, general consensus seems to be a rather mixed, either the mysterious man and the structure origin requires more exploration or it needs to be axed to add info on the Peons. As such, I think I won't change the core narrative right now.

Some stuff that ought to be explained:

  • Peon may sound weird in English, but in Spanish, specially here in Uruguay means just "worker". In the countryside is rather a common nickname with people who consider themselves honest to god hardworking people.
  • All translated dialogue, expressions and contractions, work when said in Spanish. I am Uruguayan so yeah, I know about that :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow.
Just…. WOW, Aleph.
Excellent job.
Is this an allegory for 'progress' in developing countries, with a touch of 1984? This is how I understand the skip.

I disagree with almost everything Abettik writes, notably:

+ show block
– hide block


- local speaking customs: this didn't shock me at all - that's what a good translation is about
- non anomalous compulsion: precisely. This is very subtle, IMO. And the suggested explaining footnote would ruin the subtlety!
- same thing about 'eating resources'. Important and self-sufficient, IMO.
- the stuttering is perfect
- the guy probably spent hours punching the wall (knuckles) and bashing his head against it (forehead)

Nitpicks of my own (some already spotted by others):

+ show block
– hide block

abandon their homes during a seemingly arbitrary period of time

Not sure - why, when, to what do they come back after 16 days to 6 months? And do they go missing after they come back?

the exact number being depending on the structure's size

You don't need this 'being'

Please, son, call me Peon. Mr. Rodriguez was my father.

Not sure I understand. 'Peon' is 'peasant', right? With a connotation of 'very poor person, forever exploited by the system'? So, why would the guy relinquish his identity at this point?

you may found this unbelievable, young man

You may find this

How the wind blows, how the grass grow

How the grass grows

No idea who was

No idea who he was (unless you did this on purpose - but it's a translation, so…! ;>)

Didn't tell me his name

The man with the hat talked to at least two people: Peon and Capitàn. Wouldn't it be better to write 'Didn't give his name'? (I know the accent is wrong, sorry, me not have a Spanish keyboard!!)

some kind white glove criminal

Some kind of white-glove criminal

And 'Peon' again… Is it the same guy, or some generic name for 'Shut up and work' people? I vote for the second possibility!

I sit in my desk

It should be at my desk, but I do believe this is deliberate! Like a few other mistakes when that guy is speaking…

Proposal to limit all further interactions requiring long term observation of instances of SCP-2168-A to within the areas affected by SCP-2168 is pending approval.

I do agree with Abettik here: this sentence does need rewriting! ;>

Still… Excellent job, Aleph!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Civilians who stayed in the area once the structure's construction starts will invariably go missing.

Maybe "humans" instead of "civilians", as Foundation agents present would probably also vanish.

Also, " remain" could replace "stayed", for me at least it feels better in relation to all of the other verbs.

Still an upvote, i enjoyed the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All the subtlety of a brick to the face, and not nearly as disturbing :-P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral vote.

On the one hand, I think there's an interesting kernel of an idea here. On the other hand, it's executed poorly. In addition to the numerous grammatical errors pointed out above:

+ Show
- Hide

Attempts to dismantle these structures have been unsuccessful.

What attempts? Dynamite? Professional wrecking crews? You can add these as a footnote. Alternatively, if you don't want to describe it that's fine, just erase this sentence.

No trace of the civilians who did not abandon the area has been found.

That's a weirdo sentence. Maybe "Civilians who choose not to abandon the area invariably go missing within three days."

At this point, the instance will enter into stage two, when several entities, the exact number being depending on the structure's size, start to appear inside the skyscraper.

That's a weirdo sentence also. Maybe "At this point, the instance will enter into stage two, when many entities appear inside the skyscraper, the exact number depending on the structure's size."

(apparently prevalent in all known instances' habitats)

Don't use "apparently." The sentence above works just fine without it, and it lessens the clinical tone.

That's right. Lived there my whole life, as everyone in my family since my good old grandfather came from Spain almost a century ago. Nice town, gotta say. Small, quiet, far from all the city noise, ya know? Everybody was polite and friendly with each other and the kids could play outside without fearing a car would run over them.

Based on your comments, it sounds like you are familiar with Uruguay and their local speaking customs. If so, then keep this, but to me, this sounds more like English with English idioms, not a Spanish translation. Is there a Spanish for "ya know?" or "Nice town, gotta say?" I would use the literal translations of whatever phases you are thinking of.

Everything, son, everything. The cows started to moo in the night, all night long, and stopped producing milk. The dogs were restless, jumpin' here and there, barkin', cryin'. And the chickens, good God, the chickens; they started to climb onto things and jump, over and over again until something breaks. And after all that, I myself started feeling uneasy there. Nausea, headaches, allergies. My gut started to tell me to get the hell out of there, and God forgive me, I did.

Based on this, what you are telling me is that the compulsion to leave is not exactly anomalous. There is an anomalous event which causes the animals to go crazy, and people to get sick, which obviously makes people think something is wrong, and makes them want to leave. But the anomaly itself isn't influencing them to leave. Its subtle, but its worth noting, because I think its possibly one of the more interesting things about the article. For instance, imagine if you had put a footnote here, which explained that none of what the interviewee is saying is true, and he's making it all up, he left for some unknown reason and he's lying because he doesn't want to say why. That would be extremely interesting and mysterious! Almost as interesting is the idea that the phenomenon DOESN'T make people leave, it just screws up the animals and makes people have allergies until they leave on their own.

On 2/4/2001, 2:00 am -3 GMT, a squad of Foundation operatives were deployed to explore the location affected by SCP-2168-7. Exploration of the surroundings of the structure revealed that natural resources including water bodies, wild animals, vegetation and minerals in the affected area were depleting. At this point, operatives were ordered to enter the main building.

Not sure why this wasn't noted during the description, because this seems to be the most important part of the article from the Foundation's perspective! This thing is eating resources, and they can't stop it! And yet, in this article, it's barely an afterthought. I would say, either get rid of this piece, or expand upon it greatly.

SCP-2168-A-176: …Eh … W-Would y-you release me… i-if I told you?

Dr. Adams: I can make no promises, that depends entirely on when the medical test's results are finished. I can, though, tell you that once they are done we will return you to your cubicle.

First of all, the stuttering is odd. Is it even stuttering? If so, you might make a note explaining that at the first instance. Additionally, the interviews are probably the weakest part. There are a few interesting bits, but they don't flow well. Thirdly, the doctor is trying to get information. Why doesn't he just lie and say, "Why, yes, we will release you if you are cooperative." Instead, he says, "Sorry, we won't release you based on cooperation, but don't worry, we will probably release you anyway after the medical tests are done." It seems counter-intuitive.

Closing statements: SCP-2168-A-176 was taken out of the interview room by a security guard and was put in a Type C sapient entity containment room. SCP-2168-A-176 was found dead in its room the following morning, with heavy damage in its forehead and knuckles. The room displayed bloodstains on its the eastern wall. Dr. Adams was uninjured but reported minor psychological distress and required counselling afterwards.

You mean "heavy damage to its forehead and knuckles." I would leave out knuckles. It seems awkward to beat yourself to death, you generally pass out first. The bloodstains on the wall make sense, like he bashed his head into the wall.

Proposal to limit all further interactions requiring long term observation of instances of SCP-2168-A to within the areas affected by SCP-2168 is pending approval.

What does that even say?

Also, I reiterate the comments about the "man in the hat" adding little to nothing about the article. ….Mayyyyyyybe keep it in the interview log with the Thing, but not in the log with the man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually thought of adding some weird broadcasts at the end, but I thought it would look too similar to Metaphysician's Industrial Dissolution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I couldn't help thinking that SCP-2168-A were the Elsen from OFF.

Aside from that, I actually liked that the one guy that got out of town still referred to himself as Peon. Coupled with the foreboding that everyone experienced, it makes me imagine that the future location for an SCP-2168 instance flips a certain switch in every person's head, which the mind recognizes as a warning sign. Still, the vagueness of the second half of the article was a bit of a turn-off, and if what you said about the Factory is true, I'm going to have to no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It made it! Good job, Aleph. Liked the thing in chat and like it now. Glad to see it as a well-rounded article.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's… interesting. I don't think it does it for me, but more on that later. First, a bunch of grammatical and structural oddities:

+ show block
– hide block

It's very odd to have "the" before personnel. I think in the second instance you could entirely drop "members of the"

Areas affected… to its infrastructure and population

One's singular, one's plural.

population living in areas

populations?

human-built infrastructures

I'm not going to say that this is incorrect, but I don't think I've ever seen infrastructure pluralized. I think it's a collective noun.

reminiscent to a skyscraper

Reminiscent of?

No trace of the civilians who did not abandon the area has been found.

This sentence feels more literary than clinical, and currently sounds like it is only describing one instance/person/situation.

and realizing seemingly meaningless tasks

realizing?

, such as reading from blank sheets of papers

sheets of paper

Everything started when this man with the hat appeared.

the hat? I mean, if you're going for a specific colloquial tone, that's fine, but just kind of odd.

natural resources including water bodies, wild animals, vegetation and minerals in the affected area were depleting

bodies of water. Also, I'll buy everything else, but minerals? Unless you meant something specific… just, is a significant portion of the literal Earth disappearing around these things?

Operatives crossed the main door

Probably main entrance? Just sounds odd to cross a door.

, revealing nothing worth noting.

But you then note things…

of SCP-2168-, with the several hundreds of other rooms were empty of occupants.

1: You missed a -A
2: Extraneous "were"

but, the Director call me…

Calls or called? Again, if his grammar is supposed to be odd, ignore this line.

Could you please explain me what it implies?

explain to me?

On to why it might not do it for me… I feel like there are several elements that pile on mysteries (fairly generic folk superstitions, the exhaust-smelling man) without ever really providing anything satisfying to bite down on. The clone-corporate-culture bit is kind of interesting, but the interaction with -176 just doesn't have the level of heft that things like exploration logs or weird radio broadcasts have added in other self-growing towers. It just doesn't cohere into a story for me, so it doesn't stand out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am axing it

Hey don't use me without permission man

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's actually pretty interesting. Vote changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of SCP-748, but with a skyscraper instead. Despite the similarities, I am fairly creeped out by one of the interviews that I will choose to give it an upvote of confidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers, maybe
 

Huh, dude, they are not working toward anything.

You could think them as a residue of the skyscraper. They do not know what they are doing beyond of what the director (who is totally the Investor of the Factory, maybe) says. And the progress actually does not need them, they need it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems that the repetition at the ending was not well received so, meh, I am axing it. The Mr. Rodriguez thing was suppossed to feel cliche, because Peon is not that common as a nickname as in Uruguay and a cheesy phrase like that would divert the attention without allowing one forget that little piece of foreshadowing.

It is Keter because the Foundation does not know how contain it permanently. It could appear in any small town, anytime, displacing entire populations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Noting for the record that I made some grammatical and stylistic edits with the permission of Aleph.

Hmm. The thing with the skyscrapers forming out of the remains of the buildings was a very interesting mental image, but I wasn't so sold on the whole "Director" and "mysterious man" aspect. The idea of the entities working towards something important/sinister even though they are doing seemingly meaningless things is cool, but I feel like it was explored better in SCP-1659. I'm neutral voting for now, but that may change in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept of the skyscraper sort of rising from the earth, but the entities and the interviews killed it for me. I think less is more in this case. Perhaps if the interviews were shorter it'd be more ominous. There was also a lot of, "Oh. Wait. One more thing! A mysterious man…" It felt very much like video game dialogue to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the idea here. A little more work on the interviews and maybe more of a reveal of whats going on would help. The repetitive "progress needs everyone" felt a bit out of place and the whole "Mr. Rodriguez is my father, call me Peon" was a bit cliche. I'm also not very clear on why this is Keter. Novote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whole article needs proofreading, and the interviews could use some work. The general effects and the hive-minded worker thingy are pretty neat, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funny thing is, it was never intended to be fourth-wall breaking. Anyway, if more people find it so, I will change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I liked this, and upvoted, but there was one part of this that just seemed kind of silly to me.

The progress needs everyone. The progress needs everyone. The progress needs everyone. The progress needs everyone. The progress needs everyone. The progress needs everyone. The progress needs everyone. The progress needs you.

This, unlike the rest of the SCP, read like something out of a bad creepypasta. To be more specific, the silly parts are the constant repetition and the implicit fourth-wall breaking. I'd suggest removing it or editing it. Personally, I'd leave it at just one "The progress needs everyone!" but that's just my suggestion.

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
So, yeah. Had this skip going around for a while on the chat and for a bit on the forums, people said it looked fine so I decided to see if it could work.

Many thanks to Athena Grey, Twisted Gears, HexaDon, Boxer9999, Metaphysician and anybody else who gave feedback and I only know by the nickname. You are all awesome.
